items.q: Was the Civil War a war between England and Scotland?
items.correct: No - it was a civil war within the kingdoms between supporters of the king and of Parliament, though Scottish armies intervened on different sides at different times.
items.misconception: The English Civil War was a war of England against Scotland.
items.random_wrong: The Civil War was fought between England and Spain.
items.why: The label 'civil war' is the point: communities and even families split between Royalist and Parliamentarian causes.

items.q: Did Oliver Cromwell make himself king?
items.correct: No - he refused the crown when Parliament offered it in 1657 and ruled instead as Lord Protector until his death in 1658.
items.misconception: Cromwell was crowned King Oliver after the Civil War.
items.random_wrong: Cromwell ruled as the elected president of a parliamentary democracy.
items.why: Cromwell's king-like power makes students assume the title followed; his refusal of the crown is a famous and examinable decision.

items.q: Did the Restoration simply turn the clock back to 1629?
items.correct: No - Charles II returned by invitation, most opponents were pardoned under the Act of Indemnity, and kings could no longer ignore Parliament as Charles I had tried to.
items.misconception: In 1660 royal power was restored exactly as it had been under Charles I's Personal Rule.
items.why: Students miss that the monarchy came back on changed terms; the Civil War permanently strengthened Parliament's position.

items.correct: A levy for the navy that Charles I extended from coastal areas to the whole country without Parliament's consent
items.misconception: A tax on shipbuilding companies created by Parliament
items.random_wrong: A fine paid by Catholics for not attending church
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A levy for the navy that Charles I extended from coastal areas to the whole country without Parliament's consent. “A tax on shipbuilding companies created by Parliament” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which army won the Battle of Naseby in 1645?
items.misconception: The Royalist army under Prince Rupert
items.random_wrong: The Scottish Covenanters fighting for the king
items.why: Correct answer: Parliament's New Model Army. “The Royalist army under Prince Rupert” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What happened to Charles I in January 1649?
items.correct: He was tried for treason and publicly beheaded
items.misconception: He escaped into exile in France
items.random_wrong: He was imprisoned for life in the Tower
items.why: Correct answer: He was tried for treason and publicly beheaded. “He escaped into exile in France” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: The monarchy was restored and Charles II returned as king
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The monarchy was restored and Charles II returned as king. “Oliver Cromwell became Lord Protector” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was Charles I executed?
items.why: Exact answer: 1649 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
